preamble: The shift from the pharyngeal fricative [ʕ] (Ayin) to the high-back rounded vowel [u] is a form of vocalization. Because the pharyngeal constriction lowers the second formant (F2) in a way acoustically similar to lip rounding, languages without pharyngeal consonants often substitute [u] or [o] when borrowing Semitic words.

examples.note: The pharyngeal is dropped; surrounding back vowels merge with compensatory lengthening.
Tentative: Akkadian primarily shows e-coloring (ain→e), not u-coloring. U-class vowel outcomes in specific morphological environments are less well-documented.
